exercises.title: Where the hard orchestration lives
exercises.prompt: MCP tutorials show servers and wave at clients, but client code holds the hard orchestration. Explain the client's responsibilities (process spawning, capability negotiation, multi-server tool merging, session management, reconnection) and why the client — not the server — owns this complexity.
exercises.hint: Client: spawn server processes, complete the initialize handshake, merge tool lists from multiple servers into one namespace, route calls to the owning server, manage sessions/reconnection. The client coordinates MANY servers for ONE model — that orchestration is inherently client-side.
exercises.solution:
**The client's responsibilities (the hard orchestration):**
- **Process spawning / connection:** the client **spawns each MCP server** (as a child process for stdio, or connects over HTTP) and manages its lifecycle.
- **Capability negotiation:** complete the **`initialize` handshake** with each server (Lesson 6) — send `initialize`, receive capabilities, send `notifications/initialized` — and **track what each server supports**.
- **Multi-server tool merging:** the client typically connects to **multiple servers** (a notes server, a calendar server, a database server) and must **merge their tool lists into one flat namespace** that the model sees as a single set of tools — **handling name collisions** across servers.
- **Routing:** when the model calls a tool, the client must **route the call to the server that owns it** and reassemble the response.
- **Session management:** maintain **per-server state** (capabilities, tool lists, last-seen notification ids) and handle **reconnection** when a server crashes or disconnects.
- **Client primitives:** handle **sampling callbacks** (server asks the client's LLM, Lesson 11), **elicitation** (server asks the user, Lesson 12), and **roots** (Lesson 12).

**Why the client owns this complexity:** the architecture is **one model/host coordinating MANY servers** — and that coordination is **inherently client-side**:
- **The model sees one tool namespace, but tools come from many servers.** Only the client can **aggregate** multiple servers' tools into the single namespace the model needs, and **route** each call back to the right server. A server only knows about itself; the client knows about all of them.
- **The client is the orchestrator** — it runs the agent loop, decides which servers to connect, merges their capabilities, and mediates between the model and the servers. Servers are **dumb executors** (Lesson 11); the client is the **conductor**.
- **Cross-cutting concerns (collision handling, session state, reconnection, multi-server fan-out)** can only be solved where you have the **global view** — at the client.
So while servers are relatively simple (implement your primitives, dispatch, Lesson 7), the **client is where the orchestration lives**: spawning, negotiating, merging, routing, and managing sessions across a fleet of servers for one model. That's why this lesson builds a **multi-server client** — the realistic, hard case that tutorials skip.

exercises.title: Multi-server connection, tool merging, and collision handling
exercises.prompt: Build a multi-server MCP client: spawn each server, complete initialize, merge tool lists into one namespace with collision handling, and route a tool call to the owning server. Explain the namespace-collision problem and the strategies to resolve it.
exercises.hint: Per server: spawn, send initialize, get tools/list. Merge into a flat dict tool_name -> server. Collisions: two servers expose 'search' — namespace them (server_name.tool or prefix) or disambiguate. Route by looking up which server owns the called tool name.
exercises.solution:
```python
class MultiServerClient:
    def __init__(self):
        self.servers = {}          # server_name -> session
        self.tool_owner = {}       # exposed_tool_name -> server_name

    def connect(self, name, spawn_cmd):
        session = spawn_server(spawn_cmd)                  # spawn process
        caps = session.request("initialize", CLIENT_CAPS) # handshake
        session.notify("notifications/initialized")
        tools = session.request("tools/list")["tools"]
        self.servers[name] = {"session": session, "caps": caps, "tools": tools}
        for t in tools:                                    # MERGE into namespace
            exposed = t["name"]
            if exposed in self.tool_owner:                 # COLLISION!
                exposed = f"{name}.{t['name']}"            # namespace it
            self.tool_owner[exposed] = (name, t["name"])

    def list_tools(self):                                  # flat namespace for the model
        return list(self.tool_owner.keys())

    def call_tool(self, exposed_name, args):               # ROUTE to owning server
        server_name, real_name = self.tool_owner[exposed_name]
        session = self.servers[server_name]["session"]
        return session.request("tools/call", {"name": real_name, "arguments": args})
```

**The flow:** for each server, **spawn it, complete `initialize` + `notifications/initialized`, fetch `tools/list`**, and **merge its tools into a flat namespace** (`tool_owner` maps the exposed name → which server owns it and its real name). The model sees `list_tools()` as **one combined set**; when it calls a tool, `call_tool` **routes to the owning server** by looking up the name.

**The namespace-collision problem:** when you merge tools from **multiple servers** into one namespace, **two servers may expose tools with the same name** — a notes server and a tasks server both have `search`, or two database servers both have `query`. If you naively merge them into one dict, **one silently overwrites the other** (data loss — one server's `search` becomes unreachable), or the model **can't tell them apart** and routing is ambiguous (which `search` did it mean?). This is the same collision issue as schema-design registry naming (Lesson 5), now across server boundaries.

**Strategies to resolve collisions:**
1. **Namespacing (prefix with server name):** rename colliding tools to `server_name.tool` or `server_name__tool` (e.g. `notes.search`, `tasks.search`) so each is **unique and unambiguous**, and the model can pick the right one. The most common, robust strategy (the code above does this on collision). Tradeoff: longer names, and the model sees the server structure.
2. **Prefix everything (consistent namespacing):** always prefix *all* tools with their server name (not just on collision) for **consistency and predictability** — every tool is `server.tool`, no surprises, but more verbose names.
3. **Disambiguation / priority:** if you control which servers are trusted, you could **prefer one server's tool** on collision (with a documented priority) — but this **hides** the other tool, so it's risky (the model loses access to a capability). Generally inferior to namespacing.
4. **Curation:** **don't expose colliding/overlapping tools** at all — curate which servers/tools you surface to avoid collisions and keep the namespace small (also helps tool-selection accuracy and context budget, Lessons 2, 5).
**Best practice:** **namespace on collision (or always)** so every tool is uniquely addressable and routable, keep the merged namespace **curated and not too large** (selection accuracy + context limits), and **track ownership** so routing is deterministic. Collision handling is essential the moment you connect more than one server — without it, tools silently shadow each other (a precursor to the cross-server shadowing *attack*, Lesson 15).

exercises.title: Session management, sampling/elicitation callbacks, and client robustness
exercises.prompt: Explain per-server session management (capabilities, notification ids, reconnection), how the client handles server-initiated requests (sampling, elicitation), and the robustness concerns of a multi-server client (a crashed server, a slow server, untrusted tools). How does the client mediate security and the agent loop?
exercises.hint: Session: track caps, tool list, notification ids; reconnect on crash without losing the others. Server-initiated: sampling callback runs the client's LLM; elicitation asks the user — client must handle these mid-call. Robustness: isolate failures (one crashed server shouldn't kill all), timeouts, treat tool output as untrusted. Client is the security/trust boundary.
exercises.solution:
**Per-server session management:** the client maintains **independent session state for each server**:
- **Capabilities:** what each server declared at `initialize` (which primitives/features it supports) — so the client only uses supported features per server (Lesson 6's negotiation).
- **Tool/resource/prompt lists:** cached per server, refreshed on `notifications/*/list_changed` (servers can announce their tools changed — and the client must handle this, including re-checking for collisions and **detecting unexpected changes**, which is a security signal — rug pulls, Lesson 15).
- **Last-seen notification ids:** track notifications (resource updates, list changes) per server.
- **Reconnection:** if a server **crashes or disconnects**, the client must **reconnect that server** (re-spawn, re-initialize, re-fetch tools) **without disrupting the others** — each server's session is isolated, so one failure is recoverable independently.

**Handling server-initiated requests (sampling, elicitation):** MCP is **bidirectional** (Lesson 6) — servers can send requests *to the client*:
- **Sampling (`sampling/createMessage`, Lesson 11):** a server asks the client to **run a completion through the client's LLM**. The client must **handle this callback** — receive the sampling request mid-tool-call, run it through its model (respecting `modelPreferences`), and return the completion. This lets servers host agent loops without holding model credentials — but the **client controls the model** (and should gate/inspect what it's asked to sample, a security surface — sampling attacks, Lesson 15).
- **Elicitation (`elicitation/create`, Lesson 12):** a server asks the client to **prompt the user** for input/confirmation mid-call. The client must **surface a form/prompt to the user**, collect the response, and return it. This is how a tool pauses to get a missing field or a confirmation.
The client must **handle these inbound requests during the operation phase**, not just send outbound calls — the orchestration is genuinely bidirectional.

**Robustness concerns of a multi-server client:**
- **A crashed server:** must **not bring down the whole client** — isolate each server's session, catch its failures, mark it unavailable (and reconnect), but keep serving the other servers' tools. Partial availability beats total failure.
- **A slow server:** apply **per-call timeouts** so one hung server doesn't block the agent loop; consider parallelism across servers (Lesson 3) for independent calls.
- **Untrusted tools/servers:** the client connects to **possibly-untrusted servers** (especially third-party ones from a registry, Lesson 17) — so it must treat **tool descriptions and tool results as untrusted** (poisoning, Lesson 15), **verify/pin tool hashes**, **enforce permissions**, and **not let server-returned content trigger sensitive actions** without checks. The client is where trust decisions are made.

**How the client mediates security and the agent loop:** the client is the **central trust and orchestration boundary**:
- **Security mediation:** because the client sits **between the model and all servers**, it's where you enforce **which servers/tools are allowed, hash-pinning (detect description mutations), consent for sensitive tools, sampling/elicitation gating, and treating all server content as untrusted data** (Lessons 15-18). Servers are untrusted; the client (or a gateway in front of it, Lesson 17) is the security gate. In production, much of this moves to a **gateway** (Lesson 17) that fronts the servers, but conceptually the client owns the trust boundary.
- **Agent-loop mediation:** the client **runs the agent loop** (Phase 13.1) — it presents the merged tool namespace to the model, receives the model's tool calls, **routes them to the owning servers, executes (with parallelism/streaming, Lesson 3), collects observations, and feeds them back** to the model. It also handles the **bidirectional callbacks** (sampling, elicitation) that interleave with the loop. The client is the **conductor** orchestrating model ↔ many servers ↔ user.
**The synthesis:** the multi-server MCP client is the **hard, central piece** — it spawns and negotiates with many servers, **merges their capabilities into one namespace** (with collision handling), **routes calls**, manages **per-server sessions and reconnection**, handles **bidirectional server-initiated requests** (sampling, elicitation), and acts as the **security/trust boundary** (untrusted servers, poisoning defense, consent). Servers are simple executors; **the client coordinates the whole ecosystem for one model** — which is why production deployments often **front the client with a gateway** (Lesson 17) to centralize the auth/RBAC/audit/poisoning-detection that the client would otherwise each have to implement. Building the client teaches you where all the orchestration and trust decisions actually live.
